title: Church membership as a prerequisite for employment
text:
a) Germany / b) Federal Constitutional Court / c) Second Panel / d)
29.09.2025 / e) 2 BvR 934/19 / f) Church membership as a prerequisite
for employment / g) ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2025:rs20250929.2bvr093419/ h)
Neue Zeitschrift für Arbeitsrecht 2025, 1617; Monatsschrift für
Deutsches Recht 2025, 1480; Gesundheitsrecht 2025, 701;
Entscheidungssammlung zum Arbeitsrecht-Schnelldienst 2025, Nr. 23, 10;
BetriebsBerater 2025, 331; Zeitschrift für Tarifrecht 2025, 587;
Juristische Schulung 2025, 1176; Arbeit und Recht 2025, 472.

Keywords of the systematic thesaurus:

2.2.1.6.3 Hierarchy – Hierarchy between national and non-national
sources – Law of the European Union/EU Law and domestic law – EU
secondary law and constitutions.

2.2.1.6.5 Hierarchy – Hierarchy between national and non-national
sources – Law of the European Union/EU Law and domestic law – Direct
effect, primacy and the uniform application of EU Law.

3.7 General Principles – Relations between the State and bodies of a
religious or ideological nature.

4.17.2 European Union – Distribution of powers between the EU and
member states.

5.2.2.6 Fundamental Rights – Equality – Criteria of distinction –
Religion.

5.3.18 Fundamental Rights – Civil and political rights – Freedom of
conscience.

5.3.20 Fundamental Rights – Civil and political rights – Freedom of
worship.

Keywords of the alphabetical index:

Church, employment relation, autonomy / Church, internal regulation /
Church, state law, application / Community law, principles, equal
treatment / Religion, employment / Religion, freedom.

Headnotes:

1. In principle, the Federal Constitutional Court reviews domestic
law and its application against the standard of the fundamental rights
of the Basic Law even if the domestic law in question falls within the
scope of application of EU law but is not fully determined by it. The
relevant provisions of Council Directive 2000/78/EC of 27 November 2000
(Equal Treatment Directive) regarding the scope of the right to
religious self-determination in the area of church labour law afford the
Member States latitude in the implementation of said provisions. Within
the limits set by EU law as interpreted by the Court of Justice of the
European Union (CJEU), this indicates fundamental rights diversity. As a
consequence, the balancing of interests in the area of church labour law
may lead to different outcomes in the Member States in light of their
differing constitutional standards with regard to religious
organisations.

2. The right to religious self-determination (Article 4.1 and 2 in
conjunction with Article 140 of the Basic Law and the first sentence of
Article 137.3 of the Weimar Constitution) encompasses all measures that
serve to ensure the religious dimension of the work of a religious
community and the direct link of its activities to the fundamental
mission of the religious community. This also includes legal
arrangements for the exercise of church functions in the form of the
selection of employees and the conclusion of employment contracts.

3. a) The binding requirements of Article 4.2 of the Equal Treatment
Directive, as interpreted by the CJEU, can be implemented by
interpreting the relevant national provisions in conformity with EU law.
This leads to a specification of the standards previously developed by
the Federal Constitutional Court for the two-stage assessment to be
undertaken when restrictions of the right to religious
self-determination are at issue.

b) The first stage of the assessment is clarified insofar as
effective judicial review based on the ethos of the religious community
is extended to the question to what extent the activities concerned or
the context in which they are carried out objectively give rise to a
direct link between the occupational requirement – in this case, church
membership – and the activities concerned. It falls to the religious
community to plausibly set out, on the basis of its religious ethos,
that such a link exists for the specific activities in question.

c) The overall balancing of the affected legal interests at the
second stage of the assessment is given more specific shape in that the
occupational requirement at issue must be proportionate for preserving
the religious ethos in relation to the specific activities. In view of
the openness of EU law for different fundamental right regimes in the
Member States, the courts may continue to accord particular significance
to the religious ethos due to its closeness to the religious freedom of
religious organisations (Article 4.1 and 2 of the Basic Law), which is
guaranteed without reservation.

d) The greater the significance of the affected position for the
internal or external religious identity of the religious community, the
greater the weight to be accorded to it and to the requirement of church
membership derived therefrom. The less relevant the position is for the
realisation of the religious ethos, the more the protection against
discrimination must prevail. The courts must give effect to the great
constitutional significance of the protection against discrimination in
their balancing of interests.

4. There are no insurmountable contradictions between domestic
constitutional law and EU law with regard to the scope of the right to
the self-determination of religious communities in relation to labour
law.

In accordance with the relevant guarantees of the Charter of
Fundamental Rights of the European Union and the European Convention on
Human Rights, both constitutional law and EU law generally distinguish
between impermissible theological assessments of the religious ethos by
state courts on the one hand and the restriction, in light of the rule
of law, of the exercise of the right to religious self-determination in
the area of state (equal treatment) law on the other.

Summary:

I.

The constitutional complaint concerns the question under what
conditions a church-affiliated employer may require church membership
for a specific position of employment and to what extent national courts
can review such a requirement in light of the right to religious
self-determination.

In a job advertisement for a project vacancy, the applicant, a
church-affiliated employer, stated the following: ‘We presume that
applicants are members of a Protestant church (…).’ The plaintiff, who
has no religious affiliation, applied for the position without stating
any religious affiliation. When she was not invited to a job interview,
she filed an action with the Labour Court, requesting that the applicant
pay compensation pursuant to § 15 of the General Act on Equal Treatment
(hereinafter, the “Act”) because she had been disadvantaged on religious
grounds. The Labour Court awarded compensation to the plaintiff, but
this decision was overturned by the Higher Labour Court. In the context
of the appeal on points of law filed by the plaintiff, the Federal
Labour Court requested a preliminary ruling from the Court of Justice of
the European Union (CJEU) to have the interpretation of Article 4.2 of
the Equal Treatment Directive clarified.

By judgment of 17 April 2018, the CJEU held that where a church or
organisation rejects an application for employment on the grounds that,
by reason of the nature of the activities concerned, religion
constitutes a genuine, legitimate and justified occupational
requirement, having regard to the ethos of the church or organisation,
it must be possible for such an assertion to be the subject of effective
judicial review.

The Federal Labour Court then ordered the applicant to pay
compensation to the plaintiff, holding that § 9.1 of the Act, which
provides for justification for differences of treatment of employees on
religious grounds, is incompatible with EU law in its first alternative
and therefore inapplicable, while the second alternative of § 9.1 of the
Act could also not justify the difference of treatment in this case.

II.

The Federal Constitutional Court held that the constitutional
complaint is admissible and well-founded insofar as the applicant
asserts that the application of the standards set forth by the CJEU
violates its right to religious self-determination.

The judgment of the Federal Labour Court violates the applicant’s
right to religious self-determination, because the balancing of
interests conducted in the context of the application of the second
alternative of § 9.1 of the Act does not give the constitutionally
required effect to the applicant’s right to religious
self-determination.

The Federal Labour Court did not sufficiently take into account that
Article 4.2 of the Equal Treatment Directive, as interpreted by the
CJEU, affords latitude to domestic law, within which the constitutional
requirements arising from the right to religious self-determination
apply. On this basis, the Federal Labour Court failed to accord the
constitutionally required significance to the right to religious
self-determination when balancing it against the plaintiff’s right not
to be discriminated against on grounds of her religion.

Cross-references:

Federal Constitutional Court

- 2 BvR 661/12, 22.10.2014, Entscheidungen des
Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGE 137, 273,
ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2014:rs20141022.2bvr066112.

Court of Justice of the European Union

- C-414/16, Egenberger, 17.04.2018, ECLI:EU:C:2018:257

Languages: German; English (translation by the Court
pending; press release available).

--- SUMMARY ---

1.	In principle, the Federal Constitutional Court reviews domestic law and its application against the standard of the fundamental rights of the Basic Law even if the domestic law in question falls within the scope of application of EU law but is not fully determined by it. The relevant provisions of Council Directive 2000/78/EC of 27 November 2000 (Equal Treatment Directive) regarding the scope of the right to religious self-determination in the area of church labour law afford the Member States latitude in the implementation of said provisions. Within the limits set by EU law as interpreted by the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU), this indicates fundamental rights diversity. As a consequence, the balancing of interests in the area of church labour law may lead to different outcomes in the Member States in light of their differing constitutional standards with regard to religious organisations. 

2.	The right to religious self-determination (Article 4.1 and 2 in conjunction with Article 140 of the Basic Law and the first sentence of Article 137.3 of the Weimar Constitution) encompasses all measures that serve to ensure the religious dimension of the work of a religious community and the direct link of its activities to the fundamental mission of the religious community. This also includes legal arrangements for the exercise of church functions in the form of the selection of employees and the conclusion of employment contracts. 

3. a)	The binding requirements of Article 4.2 of the Equal Treatment Directive, as interpreted by the CJEU, can be implemented by interpreting the relevant national provisions in conformity with EU law. This leads to a specification of the standards previously developed by the Federal Constitutional Court for the two-stage assessment to be undertaken when restrictions of the right to religious self-determination are at issue. 

b)	The first stage of the assessment is clarified insofar as effective judicial review based on the ethos of the religious community is extended to the question to what extent the activities concerned or the context in which they are carried out objectively give rise to a direct link between the occupational requirement – in this case, church membership – and the activities concerned. It falls to the religious community to plausibly set out, on the basis of its religious ethos, that such a link exists for the specific activities in question.

c)	The overall balancing of the affected legal interests at the second stage of the assessment is given more specific shape in that the occupational requirement at issue must be proportionate for preserving the religious ethos in relation to the specific activities. In view of the openness of EU law for different fundamental right regimes in the Member States, the courts may continue to accord particular significance to the religious ethos due to its closeness to the religious freedom of religious organisations (Article 4.1 and 2 of the Basic Law), which is guaranteed without reservation. 

d)	The greater the significance of the affected position for the internal or external religious identity of the religious community, the greater the weight to be accorded to it and to the requirement of church membership derived therefrom. The less relevant the position is for the realisation of the religious ethos, the more the protection against discrimination must prevail. The courts must give effect to the great constitutional significance of the protection against discrimination in their balancing of interests. 

4.	There are no insurmountable contradictions between domestic constitutional law and EU law with regard to the scope of the right to the self-determination of religious communities in relation to labour law. 

In accordance with the relevant guarantees of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union and the European Convention on Human Rights, both constitutional law and EU law generally distinguish between impermissible theological assessments of the religious ethos by state courts on the one hand and the restriction, in light of the rule of law, of the exercise of the right to religious self-determination in the area of state (equal treatment) law on the other. 


I.

The constitutional complaint concerns the question under what conditions a church-affiliated employer may require church membership for a specific position of employment and to what extent national courts can review such a requirement in light of the right to religious self-determination.

In a job advertisement for a project vacancy, the applicant, a church-affiliated employer, stated the following: ‘We presume that applicants are members of a Protestant church (…).’ The plaintiff, who has no religious affiliation, applied for the position without stating any religious affiliation. When she was not invited to a job interview, she filed an action with the Labour Court, requesting that the applicant pay compensation pursuant to § 15 of the General Act on Equal Treatment (hereinafter, the “Act”) because she had been disadvantaged on religious grounds. The Labour Court awarded compensation to the plaintiff, but this decision was overturned by the Higher Labour Court. In the context of the appeal on points of law filed by the plaintiff, the Federal Labour Court requested a preliminary ruling from the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) to have the interpretation of Article 4.2 of the Equal Treatment Directive clarified.

By judgment of 17 April 2018, the CJEU held that where a church or organisation rejects an application for employment on the grounds that, by reason of the nature of the activities concerned, religion constitutes a genuine, legitimate and justified occupational requirement, having regard to the ethos of the church or organisation, it must be possible for such an assertion to be the subject of effective judicial review. 

The Federal Labour Court then ordered the applicant to pay compensation to the plaintiff, holding that § 9.1 of the Act, which provides for justification for differences of treatment of employees on religious grounds, is incompatible with EU law in its first alternative and therefore inapplicable, while the second alternative of § 9.1 of the Act could also not justify the difference of treatment in this case.

II. 

The Federal Constitutional Court held that the constitutional complaint is admissible and well-founded insofar as the applicant asserts that the application of the standards set forth by the CJEU violates its right to religious self-determination.

The judgment of the Federal Labour Court violates the applicant’s right to religious self-determination, because the balancing of interests conducted in the context of the application of the second alternative of § 9.1 of the Act does not give the constitutionally required effect to the applicant’s right to religious self-determination.

The Federal Labour Court did not sufficiently take into account that Article 4.2 of the Equal Treatment Directive, as interpreted by the CJEU, affords latitude to domestic law, within which the constitutional requirements arising from the right to religious self-determination apply. On this basis, the Federal Labour Court failed to accord the constitutionally required significance to the right to religious self-determination when balancing it against the plaintiff’s right not to be discriminated against on grounds of her religion.



Federal Constitutional Court
- 2 BvR 661/12, 22.10.2014, Entscheidungen des Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGE 137, 273, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2014:rs20141022.2bvr066112. 

Court of Justice of the European Union
- C-414/16, Egenberger, 17.04.2018, ECLI:EU:C:2018:257
